Progressive myopathy and exercise intolerance starting at age 9 years, with diffuse amyotrophy, proximal weakness and axial hypotonia. 
Increased serum lactate level (3.3 mM, normal < 2.4 mM) and lactate/pyruvate ratio (25, normal < 20) at rest. Muscle histology showed abnormal presence of ragged red fibers with over 85% COX-negative fibers.
MT-TA: This is a girl presenting at 11y with exercise intolerance​, difficulty rising from floor​, waddling gait, Gower sign​, scapular winging​, muscle weakness​, diaphragmatic weakness​, and bilateral ptosis.

This is a man with young adult onset of migraines​ and kidney stones​. At 47y, he had decreased memory, muscle weakness, unsteady gait​, dysarthria​, inappropriate emotional outbursts​, hearing loss, and impaired glucose tolerance.
This is a woman with onset at 29y of progressive muscle weakness (hands, then arms and legs) who was nonambulatory by 40y​ and also had ptosis.
This is a 41y male with onset at 7-8y of progressive fatigue​, exercise intolerance (not weak​), bilateral ptosis, ophthalmoplegia​, dysarthria/nasal voice​, and spermatozoa with impaired motility​.

This is a man with onset at age 14y of exercise intolerance, difficulty climbing stairs​, proximal muscle weakness​, atrophy of lower limbs (upper limbs also but less severe​), waddling gait​, Gower sign​, weak reflexes, and lipomas.
This is a woman with onset in 50s of ptosis​, ophthalmoplegia​, dysphagia​, dysphonia​, mild proximal limb weakness​, numbness​, bilateral hearing loss
and then in her 60s experienced abnormal movements, imbalance, gait instability​, and had choreiform movements on exam.
This is a woman with onset in 60s of progressive weakness of limb girdle muscles including proximal paresis​, dysarthria​, and myopathic facies.
This is a man with onset in 40s​ of progressive muscle weakness (proximal arm/leg weakness, distal arm weakness​), exercise-induced myalgia, myalgia in arms​, and muscle wasting​.
MT-TE:
This is a 14y boy with myopathy. He developed persistent fatigue, weakness, difficulty breathing, headache, and unsteady gait after 3 days of hiking on the mountains. Exam showed facial weakness, constant facial grimaces, proximal limb weakness, unsteady gait with positive Romberg sign, dysmetria, ataxic tandem gait, and inability to hop. Brain CT and MRI were normal. He had elevated CK and lactate. Muscle biopsy showed ragged red fibers, COX-deficient fibers, and reduced activities of respiratory chain enzyme complexes I, II+III, and IV. The variant was present at 98% in muscle, 96% in urine, >99% in cheek mucosa, and 83% in blood. 

He had a 12y brother was clumsy and had decreased muscle tone, mild proximal arm weakness, and normal CK. The variant was absent in this brother's blood/urine/buccal/hair. His healthy mother had the variant present at trace levels in blood, 12% urine, 13% buccal, and trace-35% hair. His healthy 10y sister was found to have undetectable levels of the variant in blood/buccal/urine/hair.

This is a boy with mitochondrial myopathy. Onset was at birth. Skeletal muscle and liver were affected. He required tube feeding. He had clinical improvement between 10-22 months. He was last seen at age 22y and had mild proximal myopathy. Muscle  biopsy showed "profound" complex I and complex IV deficiency, ragged red fibers, COX deficiency, and ultrastructural mitochondrial changes.

This is a 55y man with diabetes and myopathy. At 14y, he had diabetes diagnosed, as well as ataxia, dysarthria, and fatigue. His diabetes was difficult to control. He developed retinopathy, cataracts, renal disease, stroke, MI, peripheral vascular disease

and motor and sensory neuropathy. Ataxia progressed restricting mobility/ He also had proximal myopathy, weakness of orbicularis oculi, and died from MI after amputation. Neurophysiology showed a mild myopathic disorder and axonal motor and sensory neuropathy.  Brain CT (53y) showed right parietal subcortical lacunar infarct and diffuse cerebral atrophy. Muscle biopsy showed subsarcolemmal accumulation of mitochondria and COX-deficient fibers. Heart biopsy showed variable COX deficiency. Skin fibroblasts showed severe reduction of complex I. The variant was present at 100% in muscle, heart, kidney, and liver
and at 94% in spleen and 92% in lung. Northern blot and protein synthesis reductions were seen. There was extensive segregation in the family.

This is a 7y girl with exercise intolerance. In early childhood, she was slower at running. At 7y, she had loss of appetite/weight loss, weakness, and slight growth retardation. On exam, she had muscle atrophy. EMG showed a myopathic pattern. Brain MRI (8y) was normal. She had slightly elevated CK. Muscle showed numerous ragged red fibers and COX-negative fibers. Electron microscopy revealed irregular mega-mitochondria with tubular structures and focally subsarcolemmal glycogen accumulations. The variant was present at 72% muscle, 38% urine, 31% blood, and 29% skin fibroblasts. Single fiber testing showed heteroplasmy levels in COX-negative fibers (N=12) to be 86% (73–96%) and in COX-positive fibers (N=12) to be 44% (11-79%)
p < 0.0001 in Student’s unpaired t-test.  The variant was absent in mother blood/urine and absent in 4 maternal aunts and MGM.

This is an individual with mitochondrial myopathy. The last assessment was at 2yo. Onset was birth. 
Skeletal muscle was the only organ affected. Muscle biopsy (performed first year of life) showed "moderate" complex I deficiency, "profound" complex IV deficiency, ragged red fibers, COX deficiency, and ultrastructural mitochondrial changes. Northern blot was performed on subsequent biopsies that showed decreased steady state levels of mt-tRNAGlu. Immunoblotting showed decrease against COX and complex I subunits.

This is a girl presenting with mitochondrial myopathy and failure to thrive. She walked in late infancy. She had persistent mild exercise intolerance. She was last evaluated at 11y and had no persistent complications. She had elevated blood lactate, CK, and CSF lactate. Muscle biopsy (5m) showed ragged red fibers. Respiratory chain enzyme testing showed reduced complex IV activity. Northern blot showed decreased mt-tRNAGLu. The variant was homoplasmic in muscle.

This is a girl presenting with congenital myopathy and muscle weakness. She walked at 2y. She was last evaluated at 15y and had no persistent complications. She had normal blood and CSF lactate. Muscle biopsy (8m) showed ragged red fibers. Respiratory chain enzyme analyses were not performed. The variant was homoplasmic in muscle.

This boy presented as “floppy infant” with muscle weakness and hypotonia from early in infancy (before 3m). By 9m, he had head control and by 18m, he walked and mild muscle weakness. CK and blood lactate were elevated and normalized after age 11m. Muscle biopsy (9m) showed ragged red fibers and decreased activities of complexes I and IV. The variant was homoplasmic in muscle.

This is a 28y man with congenital myopathy, ID, and cerebellar ataxia. Near term, he had decreased fetal movements. In infancy, he had hypotonia and fed poorly. He had global developmental delay (walk at 4y). He had poor academic performance but attended normal school. He had muscle weakness, poor coordination (never able to run or play sports), unsteady gait, and frequent falls. He had normal hearing and vision, and no seizures. He had a slowly progressive course. On exam at 28y, he was thin, had low intellect, had mild lumbar scoliosis, pectus carinatum, bilateral pes cavus, mild facial weakness
cerebellar dysarthria
globally reduced muscle bulk, moderate proximal weakness and ataxia
waddling, unsteady gait

and decreased reflexes. He had normal EEG, echocardiogram, and NCV. EMG showed mild myopathy changes. Brain MRI showed mild cerebellar atrophy. He had elevated CK, normal pyruvate, and elevated lactate. Muscle biopsy showed ragged red fibers and COX-negative fibers. Electron microscopy showed abnormal mitochondria and paracrystalline inclusions. Deficiency of complexes I and IV was seen. The variant was present at 93% in blood and 99% in muscle. His sister (IV-2) had “virtually identical” course to brother (also had primary pulmonary hypertension at 1m resolved by 1y) and the variant present at 93% blood, and 98% muscle. His mother (III-3) was healthy until  diabetes onset 45y, then also had limb weakness and exercise intolerance at 48y, and NCV showed mild axonal neuropathy. She had the variant present at 85% myoblasts, 75% blood, and 85% muscle. A healthy maternal uncle (III-2, 57y) had the variant at 65% in blood.

This is a 29y man with myopathy and diabetes mellitus. At age 27y, he had onset of exercise intolerance and progressive muscle weakness (neck and limbs). He had normal gait and mild difficulty heel walking. Reflexes were decreased in arms and brisk in legs. At age 29y, he had diabetes mellitus. EMG showed myopathic abnormalities. He had elevated CK. Muscle biopsy showed ragged red fibers, many of which were COX-deficient, as well as increased number of abnormal mitochondria with disorganization, vacuolated cristae, and paracrystalline inclusions. The variant was present at 94% in muscle, 88% WBCs, and 93% hair. Single fiber testing showed heteroplasmy levels in COX-positive fibers (n = 15) were 89.6%±9.3% and in COX-negative fibers (n = 16) were 97.4%±3.5%, P <0.004 by the Student's t-test. No family members had similar concerns. They had variant present at 21-79% in WBCs (mother - 65% WBCs, 71% hair
sister -55% WBCs, 79% hair
brother - 79% WBCs, 90% hair
mat uncle 1 - 22% WBCs, 32% hair
mat uncle 2 - 21% WBCs, 44% hair
MGM - 51% WBCs, 41% hair).

This is a 41y woman with PEO and myopathy. At 6y. she had ptosis and abnormal fundus pigmentation. She also had easy fatiguability, and muscle weakness and pain on exertion, as well as syncopal episodes thought related to hyperventilation. At 28y, on exam she had pigmentary retinopathy, PEO, and ptosis. Her migraines and muscle pain increased after pregnancies. She had reduced exercise capacity on exercise testing. She was stable from late 20s to time of report. ERG showed severely abnormal rod and cone responses. Post-exercise blood lactate (8.2mM) and pyruvate (0.2mM) were within normal published limits, although the L/P ratio (41) was slightly elevated. Muscle biopsy showed abundant ragged red fibers. Second biopsy showed COX-negative fibers. Respiratory chain enzyme activities showed marked deficiency of complex I (17% of control mean) with normal SDH and  complexes II, II+ III, III, and IV. The variant was present at 67% in muscle from 1st biopsy, 77% muscle from 2nd biopsy, undetectable blood, undetectable fibroblasts, seen in several (3/49) hair roots (het ranging from trace to 14%), and undetectable in buccal. Single fiber testing showed heteroplasmy in COX-negative fibers was 93 ± 1.0% and in COX-positive fibers was 59 ± 28%, which was noted to be highly significant (p<0.005). COX-positive fibers showed a much greater range of mutated mtDNA than COX-negative fibers (9–89% vs 92–95%). The variant was not detected in blood from the patient’s mother or three sisters (but also undetectable in proband blood).

This is a female included in a cohort study. Onset was between 1-16y. She had migraines, PEO, and pigmentary retinopathy. Muscle showed lipidosis, ragged red fibers, and COX-negative fibers. Respiratory chain enzyme activities were normal. Heteroplasmy was not specified.

This is a 45y woman with cataracts and progressive spastic paraparesis. At age 7y, she had bilateral cataracts, pigmentary retinopathy, and peripheral retinal degeneration on ERG. In her teens, she had bilateral SNHL. At 40y, she had leg pain, progressive walking difficulty, incontinence, increasing unsteadiness, and lower limb spasticity. At 44y, she had sudden-onset right sided weakness, aphasia, and dysphagia lasting several weeks. She also had behavioral disturbances, aggression, agitation, acute confusion, and disturbed sleep. On exam, she had persistent head tremor, spasticity of all 4 limbs, and brisk reflexes. At 45y, she died from bronchopneumonia. Neuropathology showed a large cystic lesion within the basal ganglia, degeneration of the pyramids, optic nerve microvacuolation, and loss of myelinated axons. She also had a severe neuronal respiratory chain complex I deficiency.

Brain MRI (40y, 44y) showed progressive cerebral atrophy and bilateral basal ganglia calcification. She had elevated blood lactate. Muscle biopsy showed COX-deficient fibers and ragged red fibers. Muscle respiratory chain enzyme activities were variable. Single fiber testing showed heteroplasmy levels in COX-deficient fibers (N=14) were 95.5% ± 3.5% and in COX-positive fibers (N=15) were 29.9% ± 31.5%, p < 0.001, 2-tailed Student t-test. The variant was present at 24% in urine (collected before death), 44% muscle (collected before death), and 7% cardiac (post mortem)
From neuropathology assessment, she had high levels in the medulla oblongata (82%) and basal ganglia (68%), whereas moderately high levels were detected in the cerebellum (58%), spinal cord (52%), pontine nuclei (51%), DRG (48%), and optic nerve (44%). The variant was absent in her healthy mother's blood and urine as well as her healthy daughter's blood and urine.

This is a 4y boy with myopathy and leukoencephalopathy. At 3m, he had an unsteady gait and hypotonia. At 1y, he had hypotonia, macrocephaly, and cerebellar ataxia. Brain MRI showed periventricular white matter hyperluciencies along with basal ganglia calcifications. Lactate was elevated. Muscle (2.5y) showed ragged red fibers and COX-negative fibers. Variant was present at 94% in muscle and 62% in blood. Single fiber testing showed heteroplasmy levels in COX-negative fibers (N=20) was 95.8% ± 1.2 and in COX-positive fibers (N=17) was 85.8% ± 2.7
significant (p < 0.01). The variant was absent in blood from the asymptomatic mother, sister, and two maternal aunts.

This girl presented as “floppy infant” with muscle weakness and hypotonia from early in infancy (before 3m) and had poor suck. By 9m, she had head control. By 16m, she walked. By 4y, she had no clinical signs of disorder. Brain MRI (23m) showed abnormally high T2- weighted signals in bilateral caudate nuclei and putamina. This was not seen on prior scan at 7m. She had elevated CK and blood lactate that normalized after age 35m. The variant was homoplasmic in lymphocytes.

This is a 63y woman with CPEO and myopathy. In early childhood, she had bilateral eyelid droop. At 
38y, she had worsening ptosis, and neck and proximal upper limb ache. At 51y, she had neck and proximal upper limb weakness. On current exam, she had ophthalmoplegia, bilateral ptosis, facial weakness, and mild proximal limb weakness. EMG showed myopathic features. CK was normal. Muscle biopsies taken 11 years apart revealed a progressive increase in the proportion of cytochrome c oxidase (COX)-deficient fibres (1997: 1-2% COX-deficient fibers

2008: 7% COX-deficient fibers, many of which were ragged red fibers). The variant was undetectable in blood, undetectable in buccal, and undetectable in urine. It was present in muscle (7% first biopsy, 34% second). Single fiber testing showed levels of the variant in COX-deficient fibers (N=10) to be 99.7± 0.5% and in COX-positive fibers (N=8) to be 33.7± 27.8%

statistically significant finding (P<0.001, two-tailed Student's t test). Her sister with MS had undetectable levels of the variant in blood, urine, buccal. Her healthy 39y and 41y daughters had undetectable levels of the variant in blood, urine, buccal, muscle.

This is a 47y man with diabetes and myopathy. At 30y, diabetes was diagnosed. He also had severe myopathy that led to job loss. He was later found to have mild pigmentary epithelium alteration in macular area and high frequency hearing loss. Muscle biopsy showed a large number of ragged red fibers and reduced CIV activity. The variant was present at 65% in blood, 78% in muscle

and later reported to be 71% in fibroblasts, 82% in muscle, 61% in WBC, 65% in hair, and 69% in buccal.

His mother had diabetes and died at 60y after 2 stroke-like episodes. His two brothers (II-6, II-7) had diabetes. His sister (II-5) had glucose intolerance and mild high frequency HL, and the variant present at 29% blood. His healthy sister (II-1) had the variant at 31% blood
her healthy daughter (III-1) had the variant at 3% blood, and III-1’s healthy daughter had the variant at 24% blood.
MT-TF: Case with Gitelman syndrome (most common hereditary salt-losing tubulopathy characterized by hypokalemic alkalosis and hypomagnesemia).  Common clinical manifestations include muscle cramps, paresthesias, nocturia, salt-craving, muscle weakness, and fatigue.
This is an individual with tubulointerstitial kidney disease.

Case with Gitelman syndrome (most common hereditary salt-losing tubulopathy characterized by hypokalemic alkalosis and hypomagnesemia).  Common clinical manifestations include muscle cramps, paresthesias, nocturia, salt-craving, muscle weakness, and fatigue.

Case with Gitelman syndrome (most common hereditary salt-losing tubulopathy characterized by hypokalemic alkalosis and hypomagnesemia).  Common clinical manifestations include muscle cramps, paresthesias, nocturia, salt-craving, muscle weakness, and fatigue.
This is a girl with severe epilepsy. At 10m, she had her first epileptic seizure, left-sided complex partial seizure followed by transient paresis of left arm

started AED, but 2m after stopped due to adverse effects, had second seizure (2y). At 2y, with second seizure, she developed vision loss lasting several weeks and developmental delay. She had persistent intractable seizures over next couple years and progressive encephalopathy with tetraplegia. She had chronic renal insufficiency. At age 17y, she died of heart failure after status epilepticus. Brain MRI was normal. Lactate was normal. Muscle biopsy showed COX-staining decreased in all fibers. Respiratory chain enzyme activities were as follows: CI: 28.7% controls (CI/CS: 60% controls)
CII: not done
CIII: not done
CIV: 14.7% control (CIV/CS: 43% controls)
CS: 41.8% controls. The variant was homoplasmic in muscle, urine, fibroblasts, and blood. The variant was present at heteroplasmy in buccal from brother, mother, and MGM.

Case with Gitelman syndrome (most common hereditary salt-losing tubulopathy characterized by hypokalemic alkalosis and hypomagnesemia).  Common clinical manifestations include muscle cramps, paresthesias, nocturia, salt-craving, muscle weakness, and fatigue.

This is a boy with adolescent onset severe epilepsy. At 15y, after a few days with headache, he presented with 2 generalized tonic-clonic seizures rapidly followed by continuous partial seizures affecting left hemiface

status stopped 72h after intensive barbiturate treatment requiring mechanical ventilation. At 17y, he had additional intractable seizures and died in ICU of infection after being in ICU for 4m in status epilepticus. Muscle biopsy showed COX-staining decreased in all fibers and respiratory chain enzyme activities: CI: 77.2% controls (CI/CS: 40% controls), CII: 125% control, CIII: 92.5% control (CIII/CS: 50.6% controls), CIV: 44.6% control (CIV/CS: 24.3% controls), CS: 177% controls. The variant was "almost homoplasmic" in muscle and homoplasmic in blood. Haplogroup is T2.

This is a 17y girl with mitochondrial myopathy. At 14y, she had breathlessness and heart palpitations with exertion, this progressed to include muscular fatigue. She had mild generalized muscle weakness (most severe in ankle dorsiflexors and abductors, 60% normal). She had pale optic discs, narrow retinal vessels, and salt and pepper retinopathy. ERG showed almost extinguished cone and low rod responses. She had elevated blood, urine, and CSF lactate and mildly elevated CK. Muscle biopsy showed type 2 fiber atrophy, increased content of fat, occasional ragged red fibers, and fibers with reduced or absent COX staining. The variant was present at 79% in muscle and undetectable in blood and fibroblasts. Single fiber testing showed heteroplasmy levels in COX-negative fibers (N=11) to be 78±22% and in COX-positive fibers (N=15) to be 45±16%, P < 0.001.

This is a 32y woman with MELAS. She had normal development until 12y. At this time, she had an acute episode of headache, photophobia, vomiting, and left arm focal motor fitting then recovered​.  She then experienced two further episodes

after the second, she developed left hemiplegia which resolved​. She then had progressive cognitive decline, cerebellar ataxia, and complex partial seizures​. Upon exam at 29y, she had salt and pepper retinopathy​, cerebellar ataxia​. spasticity​, and intermittent dystonic posturing of upper limbs with walking. Brain CT showed extensive low density areas involving both grey and white matter, most marked posteriorly

consistent with areas of infarction. She had elevated blood lactate. Muscle (performed at 12y) showed ragged red fibers that were all COX-positive. The variant was present at 58% in muscle and undetectable in blood and fibroblasts. The variant was also undetectable in her mother's blood.
This is an individual with tubulointerstitial kidney disease and no evidence of extra-renal disease.

This is a 66y woman with myopathy and neuropathy. She was previously a hiker. At age 62y, she had walking difficulty due to exercise intolerance/fatigue and paresthesia in feet. She had an unsteady gait, weakness climbing stairs, muscle cramps, paresthesias in both feet up to the knees, and mild hearing impairment. EMG showed mild neurogenic changes. Muscle biopsy showed several atrophic fibers, COX-deficient fibers (>35%), and 15% showing subsarcolemmal mitochondrial accumulation. EM showed enlarged mitochondria with paracrystalline inclusions. When respiratory chain complex activities were expressed relative to the activity of citrate synthase, the activities of complex I (50%), complex III (68%), and complex IV (55%) were shown to be decreased as compared with the lower normal value of controls. She had the variant present at 88% in muscle, 70% hair, 66% urine, 63% buccal, 36% blood. Single fiber testing showed the variant present in COX-negative (N=11) at 93%±1.0 and in COX-positive fibers (N=14) at 82%±8.5
P<0.001.

This is a 70y woman with mitochondrial myopathy. She has had lifelong slightly impaired balance​. At 50y, she had progressive bilateral ptosis​. At 67y, she underwent surgery for tachycardia​. At 68y, she had leg weakness, fatigue, and soreness after activity​.  At 70y, her exam showed ophthalmoplegia
neck flexion/hip flexion/knee extension weakness

and slow repetitive hand and feet movements​. Neuropsychological testing showed impaired memory for verbal material, reduced visual attention (encephalopathy). Brain MRI showed small bilateral lesions compatible with ischemic areas. She had elevated blood, urine, and CSF lactate. Muscle biopsy showed ragged red fibers and COX-negative fibers​. Respiratory rates and enzyme activities revealed that pyruvate+malate was 88% controls​
succinate+rotenone was 88% controls​
ascorbate+TMPD was 99% controls​, COX was 51% controls​l NADH ferricyanide reductase was135% controls​
succinate-cytochrome c reductase was 90% controls​

and citrate synthase was 231% controls. The variant was present at 70% heteroplasmy in muscle and undetectable in hair and blood. Single fiber testing showed a mean heteroplasmy of 86% in COX-negative fibers and 15% in COX-positive fibers.

This is a 14y boy with nonprogressive weakness and myalgia. He had normal developmental milestones. At age 8y, he had muscle pain, cramping, and fatigue after short walks. He had abdominal pain, headache, and vomiting after physical exertion. He had reduced muscle bulk, reduced muscle strength, and trunk and girdle weakness. He had brisk reflexes. Brain MRI was normal. He had elevated blood lactate. Muscle biopsy showed numerous ragged red fibers, COX-negative fibers, and lipid and glycogen storage. In muscle, he had an elevated CS, CI/CS at 25% controls, CIII/CS 13% controls, and CIV/CS 8% controls. He had the variant at >90% in muscle, 40% urine, and 4% blood. The variant was absent in his mother's blood and urine.

This is a 36y man from Western Turkey with mitochondrial myopathy. In his mid 20s, he had muscle weakness and pain. At 30y, he had repeated bronchial infections, fall. At age 35y, he had pain, exercise-induced dyspnea, and vertigo. He had fatigue, easy exhaustion, general mild muscle weakness and pain (especially in arms and thorax), and positive Gowers. Overall, this was a gradually progressive muscle weakness. Brain MRI showed mild diffuse atrophy. He had severe metabolic acidosis and elevated lactate. Muscle biopsy showed ragged red fibers and aggregated subsarcolemmal mitochondria which presented with typical intramitochondrial paracrystalline type I inclusions located in intracristal space. He had reduced complex I activity. The variant was present at 95% in muscle and 20% in blood.

This is a 57y woman with progressive neurodegeneration. She was referred to psychiatric services due to self-neglect, poor mobility, and weight loss. Prior she had worked as a secretary. In her mid 30s, she was in a motor vehicle accident causing LOC and flail ribs but made full recovery. In her mid 40s, she had worsening HL (on exam had profound SNHL L>R). In her late 40s, she had progressive worsening mobility and increasing falls. In her mid 50s, she had facial grimacing and was able to walk only short distances holding onto furniture with stopped posture. She had deteriorating short-term memory and word-finding. She had paranoid delusions and auditory hallucinations.  She had limb weakness and quadriceps muscle wasting. She had spastic legs, brisk reflexes, and bilateral upgoing plantar responses. She had ataxia. Brain MRI showed generalized cerebral, cerebellar, and brainstem atrophy. She had normal blood lactate, elevated CSF lactate, elevated CSF protein, and mildly elevated CK (164 U/L). Muscle biopsy showed ragged red fibers and COX-deficient fibers. The variant was present at 85% in muscle, 29% in urine, and 3% in blood. Single fiber testing showed heteroplasmy levels in COX-negative fibers (N=11) to be 99.4% [0.2%] and in COX-positive fibers (N=9) to be 72.1% [8.5%]

This is a 14y girl  with epileptic encephalopathy. At 8.5y, she had headaches, dizziness, and easy fatiguability. One month later, she developed recurrent episodes of vomiting and fever and unusual gait. At 9y, she had seizures. At 10.5y, she had an episode of severe headache and vomiting with loss of consciousness

required intubation due to metabolic acidosis. She was able to be weaned from ventilator but lost all psychomotor skills and is in vegetative state. Brain MRI showed diffuse cortical atrophy. She had elevated lactate. Assays in skin fibroblasts showed a lower mitochondrial membrane potential and ineffective mitochondrial electron transport chain to support oxidative phosphorylation. Patient fibroblasts displayed profound respiratory deficiency. The variant was present at 78% in urine, 80% in fibroblasts, and 24% in blood. Mother and grandmother had migraines. The variant was absent in blood and urine from mother, grandmother, and sister.

This is a 65y woman with ataxia, PEO, and deafness, as well as moderate ID. In her 50s, she had PEO, muscle weakness with proximal wasting (especially upper girdle and neck), gait ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, urinary incontinence, and SNHL. On exam, she had absent DTRs and reduced vibratory sensation

dysmetria, positive Romberg, and ataxia. Muscle biopsy showed ragged red fibers and COX-negative fibers. The variant was present at 80% in muscle. Single fiber testing showed levels of the variant in COX-negative (N=8) fibers to be 97% and in COX-positive fibers (N=7): to be 40%, P=2.89e-04.
This is a boy from Jiangxi Province in China with chronic kidney disease. At 12y, he was diagnosed with chronic kidney disease and hyperuricemia. The variant was present at homoplasmy.

This is a 42y Italian woman with MERRF. At 11y, she had panic attacks. In her 20s, she had migraines and progressive limb myoclonus. In her 30s, she had exercise intolerance, imbalance, and memory problems. At 37y, she had progressive SNHL. At 42y, on exam, she had short stature, pes cavus, partially restricted upgaze
slight leg spasticity, mild weakness of neck flexors and quads against resistance
frequent myoclonic jerks, bilateral clonus at patella and ankle, cerebellar signs (dysmetria/tremor on finger-nose testing and heel-shin)
dysarthria, mild hand tremor

and poor balance with tandem gait and positive Romberg. She had mild impairment on cognitive testing. She had a normal echo and EKG: EEG showed generalized spikes and spike and wave complexes with background slowing. NCV was normal. EMG showed mild myopathic features. Brain MRI showed moderate cerebral and more severe cerebellar atrophy. Brain MRS showed decreased choline peak but no lactate peak. She had increased resting blood lactate (3.12 mmol/L, normal 0.45-2.45). Muscle showed abundant ragged red fibers (RRF) and COX-negative fibers. The variant was present at 91% in muscle and undetectable in blood, urine, skin fibroblasts, and buccal sample. Single fiber testing showed the variant present in COX-negative RRF (N=10) at 93.3%±4 and in COX-positive nonRRF (N=10) at 17.4%±7.3.

Case with Gitelman syndrome (most common hereditary salt-losing tubulopathy characterized by hypokalemic alkalosis and hypomagnesemia).  Common clinical manifestations include muscle cramps, paresthesias, nocturia, salt-craving, muscle weakness, and fatigue.
This was a 7y boy from Jiangxi Province in China. At age 2y, he had transient salivation, aphasia, and left upper extremity weakness
resolved. Then around this time, chronic kidney disease was noted. The variant was present at homoplasmy. Haplogroup was F2.

Case with Gitelman syndrome (most common hereditary salt-losing tubulopathy characterized by hypokalemic alkalosis and hypomagnesemia).  Common clinical manifestations include muscle cramps, paresthesias, nocturia, salt-craving, muscle weakness, and fatigue.

This is a 73y woman with severe paraspinal muscle atrophy and cerebellar ataxia. At 63y, she had slight stopping posture and pushed out waist. At 68y, she needed walking stick due to unstable gait and was diagnosed with hypothyroidism (no improvement on treatment). At 70y, she had difficulty climbing stairs. At 71y, she had prominent paraspinal muscle atrophy and mild proximal limb muscle weakness on exam

also had right ptosis, dysarthria, bilateral cataracts, high frequency SNHL
Moderate neck flexion weakness, mild proximal muscle weakness. She also had camptocormia (involuntary trunk flexion when erect that disappears when supine), “bent spine syndrome

” axial myopathy – selective involvement of paraspinal muscles. CT of T- spine showed severe atrophy and fatty degeneration of paraspinal muscles, EMG showed mild myopathic features, brain MRI showed moderate cerebellar and temporo-parieto-occipital lobe atrophy, and brain MRS showed absence of lactate peak. She had elevated CK and normal blood and CSF lactate. Muscle biopsy showed many ragged red fibers and COX-negative fibers along with abnormal mitochondria with paracrystalline inclusions. The variant was present at 64.7% in muscle and undetectable in blood.

This is an 11y Japanese girl with epilepsy and hearing loss as well as short stature. At age 6y, she had hearing difficulty noted by family. At age 8y, she was seen by ENT, prescribed hearing aides. She also had generalized tonic-clonic seizures. At age 10y, on exam, she had hypertrichosis, exercise intolerance, and was unable to communicate verbally but could communicate in writing. She had improved hearing with cochlear implant. Brain MRI noted diffuse mild brain atrophy. She had elevated blood and CSF lactate. Muscle biopsy showed COX-negative fibers. The variant was present at 80% in muscle, 80% in skin, and 70% in blood.

Case with Gitelman syndrome (most common hereditary salt-losing tubulopathy characterized by hypokalemic alkalosis and hypomagnesemia).  Common clinical manifestations include muscle cramps, paresthesias, nocturia, salt-craving, muscle weakness, and fatigue.

This is a 6.5y girl from Anhui Province in southern China with chronic kidney disease and who received renal transplant at 7.5y with subsequent normal glomerular and tubular function. The variant was homoplasmic in blood, urine, and skin fibroblasts. Haplogroup was D4.

Case with Gitelman syndrome (most common hereditary salt-losing tubulopathy characterized by hypokalemic alkalosis and hypomagnesemia).  Common clinical manifestations include muscle cramps, paresthesias, nocturia, salt-craving, muscle weakness, and fatigue.

This is a 25y woman with CPEO and myopathy who also had ptosis, diplopa, dysphagia, and exercise intolerance. Muscle biopsy showed ragged red fibers and COX-negative fibers. The variant was present at 76% in muscle and undetectable in blood and urine.  Single fiber testing showed levels of the variant in COX-negative fibers (N=20) at87.4%±6.8 and in COX-positive fibers (N=20) at 22.4%±7.5, P<0.0001.

This is a5y girl with developmental delay, chronic renal insufficiency and epilepsia partialis continua (EPC). At age 3y, she had status epilepticus, evolved into EPC. At age 5y, she had chronic renal failure (stages II-III) due to tubulointerstitial kidney disease. She had mild respiratory compensated metabolic acidosis. Renal US showed hyperechogenic kidneys with normal length on the right side and an enlarged left kidney bearing a parenchymal bridge. Brain MRI revealed cortical edema of the right parieto-occipital region. The variant was homoplasmic in blood and urine. Her healthy mother had the variant at 91% blood, 82% urine, and 86% buccal.

This is a 43y woman with childhood onset mitochondrial myopathy. She has static cerebellar ataxia, exercise intolerance, and retinitis pigmentosa. Muscle biopsy showed many ragged red fibers, COX-negative fibers, and lipid overload. Respiratory chain enzyme activities in muscle were normal. She had the variant at 92% in muscle and it was undetectable in blood, buccal, and urine. She was in haplogroup H80. Single fiber testing showed the variant was present in COX-negative fibers (N=at least 10) at 89.10±9.3% and in COX-positive fibers (N=at least 10) at 44.74±24.79, P<0.0001.  The variant was present in her unaffected mother at <5% in blood and undetectable in urine and buccal sample, and the variant was undetectable in her healthy sister's blood, urine, and buccal samples.
MT-TH: Patient developed bilateral cataracts and hearing loss in childhood. At the age of 20, he had also developed retinitis pigmentosa, dysarthria, muscle hypotrophy and hypotonia. He was notable for hypogonadism and a prepubertal aspect. Serum lactate normal. Cranial MRI revealed hypotrophy of the cerebellar vermis, enlargement of the fourth ventricle, and bilateral foci of high T1 signal intensity in the basal ganglia and cerebellar dentate nuclei. Muscle histology revealed ragged red fibers and COX-negative fibers (Fig. 1). MRC enzyme activity in muscle was supposedly normal -- must be a mistake. Heteroplasmy determined to be 87.2% in muscle and 38.3% in blood using RFLP (Fig. 2C).

Male proband, 9 years onset, overweight, excessive sweating, and suspected hypogonadism.
At 16 years: progressive neurological decline, over the previous 7 years he had become clumsy, continued to be overweight (BMI 29.0), genitals were prepubertal, and the patient seemed psychomotorically retarded and bradykinetic.
At 17.5 years (time of report). Neurological examination now revealed dysarthria, clumsiness, limb rigidity, and bradykinesia. 
MRI: Brain MR imaging at age 16 years revealed subacute, bilateral abnormalities in the putamina and the caudate nuclei were oedemic (Fig. 1A).
Thirteen months later, chronic, necrotic changes were seen in the same brain regions (Fig. 1B). The imaging findings suggested a Leigh-like mitochondrial disorder.
Normal lactate Lactate levels CSF 2.4 mmol/l (laboratory reference, 0.6–2.7 mmol/l)., serum: (1.0 mmol/l).

At 16 years of age the proband suddenly developed blurred vision, diplopia, headache, vertigo with frequent falls, and generalised weakness and malaise. At the same time she gained more than 10 kg (body mass index = 30 kg/m2 ) in only a few weeks and was found to be hypertensive with impaired glucose tolerance. Up-beat nystagmus with bilateral gaze evoked horizontal nystagmus and a right VIth nerve palsy. Optokinetic nystagmus was severely disturbed in both directions.
Elevated CSF lactate: (2.8 mM, normal values ,1.2 mM), serum lactate normal range
Impaired glucose tolerance, hyperlipidaemia due to high blood triglycerides, and hyperuricaemia
MRI: symmetric areas of abnormal signals in the lenticular nuclei, particularly in the caudate nucleus, and also a prominent lesion in the left putamen (fig 2A). In infratentorial sections, the magnetic resonance image displayed symmetric lesions in the mesencephalon, a prominent lesion of the left cerebellar hemisphere and bilateral lesions of the superior cerebellar peduncles (fig 2B). 
MRS lactate peak
Muscle biopsy histology: normal: no ragged red fibres or cytochrome c oxidase depleted fibres were detected. Skin biopsy: reduced complex I and IV (complex 2 unaffected).
Family history:
Subject II-2 Sister: similar phenotype with and age of onset at 6 years, she died at age 21 years. The autopsy confirmed the presence of a subacute necrotising encephalopathy with spongy necrosis, vascular proliferation and gliosis in the cerebellum, basal ganglia and thalamus. There was also bilateral optic neuropathy with round cell infiltrates. The skeletal muscle only showed non-specific changes (that is, increased fibre size variation and a few atrophic fibres). No ragged red fibres or cytochrome c oxidase depleted fibres were detected.
Subject II-3, elder sister died at 1 year of age, autopsy report, symmetric spongy degeneration of the brainstem was noted, together with marked telangiectatic vascular abnormalities in the mesencephalon.
Mother: asymptomatic at age 55 years, normal brain MRI and metabolic analysis.

The proband had a history of feeding difficulties in early infancy as well as delayed development. At 16 months she was profoundly hypotonic and lethargic with poor respiratory effort. 
MRI: T2 signal bilaterally and symmetrically within the posterior midbrain, including periaqueductal gray matter, extending inferiorly to involve the pons and medulla to a lesser degree, cerebral underdevelopment, prominence of the extra-cerebral CSF spaces, and thin corpus callosum.
MRS lactate peak in basal ganglia. 
CSF studies showed elevated lactate of 3.4 mmol/L (normal 1.1–2.8), serum lactate 32.8 mg/dL (normal 4–16).
MRI one year after discharge revealed interval progression of the changes within the dorsal midbrain, pons, and medulla as well as evolution of high T2 signal within the putamen and caudate heads and bodies bilaterally.
6 years at report: able to sit independently, but is nonverbal and not able to grasp objects with her hands. She continues to be G-tube and tracheostomy dependent.
Patient fibroblasts: After normalization to the elevated citrate synthase the activities of rotenone sensitive complex I+ III (33%) and complex IV (30%) were significantly reduced. 
Haplogroup A

Neurological phenotype: The patient presented at 10 years of age with a 3-year history of tremors and unsteady gait followed by progressive weakness and voice changes. On exam she was cognitively intact with hypertelorism, frontal bossing, vertical ophthalmoplegia, generalized weakness (proximal greater than distal), athetoid movements of the fingers, areflexia, truncal ataxia, dysmetria, lower extremity atrophy, and ataxic gait.
Initial MRI: Symmetric T2 hyperintensities in medulla and pons. Repeat MRI: Progression of lesions in the tectum, oculomotor nuclei, and cerebral peduncles, pons, and superior cerebellar peduncles.

Muscle biopsy: revealed type I fiber predominance with normal spectrophotometeric analysis.

The proband showed some developmental delay, at the age of 20 months his level of development was that of an 11-month old child.  At the age of 20 months he was admitted to the hospital due to loss of appetite, tachypnoea and worsening of general condition which developed during the treatment of rhinitis. Somnolence, loss of muscle tone and dehydration were observed. Examination revealed insufficient body mass, lowered muscle tone (the boy was not walking), thinning of the skin and increased perspiration. Emotional contact and comprehension were good but the boy was not talking.
Laboratory tests showed lactic acidaemia (40-82 mg/dl) and hyperalaninaemia (516 μmol/l

normal value 186-318 μmol/l). The proband died at 28 months.
No brain MRI, but post mortem neuropathological lesions were typical for LS.
"Fig4. Microscopic view of post mortem brain stem lesions typical for LS. Vascular proliferation, spongy degeneration, neuronal damage and small pseudocyst formation. Macroscopically the central nervous system showed symmetrical focal grey discolorations localized in the basal ganglia and pons, similar more distinctly outlined ones in the dorsal part of the medulla approximately 0.4 cm in diameter, and bilateral delicate discolorations in the vicinity of the dentate nuclei. Microscopically the above foci showed spongy degeneration, astroglial and capillary proliferation as well as neuronal damage. In several foci central microcystic areas had developed. "

Neuropathological confirmation of Leigh syndrome. 
The proband had a progressive myoclonus and ataxia: At the age of 7 years he developed occasional myoclonic jerks affecting first the limbs and later the head. By the age of 10 years
the jerks had become more frequent but tended to occur in clusters separated by up to a week. He also had additional brief atonic seizures. Aged 17 his speech was slurred, his handwriting unintelligible. On examination at age 17 years he had a slurring dysarthria and normal hearing. Pursuit eye movements were jerky, with hypometric saccades in the vertical plane. There were occasional myoclonic jerks in the limbs, with mild upper limb ataxia. Power was normal. The tendon reflexes were brisk with ankle clonus and extensor plantar responses. Joint position and vibration sense were impaired in the toes. Romberg's sign was positive and his gait was broad based. Progression and severe disease were evident by 18 years, he developed dysphagia and pneumonia, requiring ventilation and nasogastric feeding.
Computerised tomography showed cerebellar atrophy.
Elevated lactate: Plasma and CSF lactate concentrations were 4.1 and 2.9 mmol/l (normal < 1.8). CSF protein was 0.27 g/I.
MRI scan showed marked atrophy of the posterior fossa structures, which was present in a milder degree in the cerebral hemispheres. A striking high signal lesion was seen to extend along the posterior surface of the brainstem from the level of the dorsal column nuclei to the midbrain.
A second MRI scan showed rostral extension, in continuity, of the high signal lesion which now involved the thalamus (Fig. 1).
Muscle biopsy: no Ragged red fibres, A high proportion of fibres showed very low or negligible cytochrome oxidase activity. Electron microscopy showed the presence of large intermyofibrillar neutral lipid droplets in about 15% of fibres. Occasional mitochondria lacked internal cristae.
Neuropath: see text for full notes

Histochemical analyses revealed ragged red fibers (RRF) and decreased cytochrome oxidase activity.

normal <20). Muscle histology revealed both ragged red and COX-negative fibers (Fig. 1). Complex IV deficiency was observed (33.6% compared to the reference range median, Table 1).

This is a 51y Finnish woman. She was less athletic than peers. In adulthood, she had poor muscle fitness, exercise-induced muscle weakness, and myalgia, along with mild dysphagia and choking tendencies. In her 40s, she had ptosis. She had normal blood lactate. Muscle biopsy showed ragged red fibers and COX-negative fibers. The variant was homoplasmic in muscle and blood.

This is a 64y Finnish man with myopathy. He was healthy for most of his life. At 54y, he developed bilateral ptosis, diplopia, and exercise intolerance. He had right leg muscle atrophy with restricted movements. On exam, he had bilateral vertical gaze paresis and a slight restriction in horizontal movements. Ankle reflexes were absent.Brain MRI showed nonspecific white matter lesions in the frontal lobe. EMG showed polyphasic units in frontal and nasal muscle. Myasthenia gravis was first diagnosed and standard treatment did not help. He had a normal CK, lactate and slightly elevated pyruvate (84 umol/L, nl 30-80). Muscle (vastus lateralis) biopsy showed COX-negative fibers (10%), ragged red fibers, and ultrastructural examination revealed an increased number of mitochondria and changes in the internal structure of mitochondria. Variant was present at 33% muscle, 2% buccal, and undetectable in blood. Single fiber testing showed COX-negative fibers had heteroplasmy of 92% and normal fibers were 43%.

This is a 40y woman with LHON and MS. She had bilateral and painless vision loss and dyschromatopsia associated to 2 months of paresthesia in lower limbs. She was a heavy smoker/drinker. Exam showed numbness in the sole of feet, hypopalestesia in both malleolus, positive Romberg test and unsteady tandem gait. Visual improvement occurred over several years. MRI showed multiple supra- and infra-tentorial white matter lesions, right optic nerve atrophy and optic chiasm and tracts anomalies. The spinal cord MRI also showed multiple focal hyperintense lesions at C2, T5, T7 and T9. Oligoclonal bands were present in CSF. The variant was present at homoplasmy.
This is a 15y Caucasian boy with LHON.

This is a girl with MERRF.  At 6y, she had bilateral hearing impairment, progressed beginning around 8y, and cochlear implant at 10y. Also at 10y, short stature was noted along with pigmentary retinopathy. At 12y, she had myoclonic seizures and generalized seizures. MRI and CT scans of the head revealed calcification of the bilateral dentate nuclei, caudate nuclei, putamina, globi pallidi, and thalami. She had elevated lactic acid/pyruvic acid in the blood (23.6/0.87 mg/dL) and spinal fluid (35.0/1.29 mg/dL). Muscle (12y) biopsy showed scattered COX-negative and ragged red fibers. The variant was homoplasmic in muscle.

This is a Caucasian girl with MERRF. In the first 8 years of life, she had exercise intolerance, several episodes of abdominal pain, vomiting, and generalized seizures. Between 9-11y, she had bilateral SNHL, recurrent migraines, and cognitive delay/regression, along with pigmentary retinopathy. Between 12-13y, she had epilepsy with frequent myoclonic jerks, decreased facial expressions, muscle weakness, exercise intolerance, ataxia, dysmetria, and mild dysarthria. Brain MRI (9y) showed cortical and cerebellar atrophy. Brain MRI (13y) showed globus pallidus changes. She had elevated blood lactate and alanine. Muscle biopsy showed ragged red fibers, COX-negative fibers, and electron microscopy showed subsarcolemmal accumulations of large mitochondria with alteration of the cristae.

This is a 16y boy with central nervous system involvement and myopathy. At 2y, he had generalized convulsions accompanied by headaches and vomiting. At 9y, he had progressive hearing loss and school performance at the lowest level (IQ 70). At 13y, he was noted to have ptosis, muscle wasting. 
On exam, he had short stature (-4.9 SD) and poor weight gain (-3.4 SD), and hypogonadism, as well as proximal muscle weakness and wasting and myoclonus in the upper and lower extremities. Brain CT showed generalized cerebral atrophy and calcifications in the putamen, globus pallidus, caudate head, and dentate nucleus. He had high lactate in blood (2.5 mmol/L

normal 0.5-2) and CSF (3.8 mmol/L, normal 0.5-2). Muscle biopsy (biceps) showed marked type 2b fiber atrophy, ragged red fibers, and focal COX deficiency. Variant was present at 74% muscle, 32% skin fibroblasts, and 18% blood.

MRI head showed symmetrical high signal lesions on T2 in the supraventricular white matter. Similar changes were also seen in the corupus callosum, caudate, putamen and globus pallidus and crus cerebri. 
Serum and CSF lactate was elevated.

Developmental delay
At 4 years he was found to have global developmental delay, at ten years old, he was documented to have fatigue, intellectual disability, bradyphrenia, hypokinetic with dystonic posturing, and unilateral resting tremor. He also exhibited an abnormal breathing pattern with frequent sighing as well as having speech and swallowing difficulties. 
He had a CT scan which showed hypointensities in the putamen and globus pallidus. His serum lactate was mildly elevated at 2.5mmol/L (normal < 2 mmol/L). His clinical phenotype was compatible with Leigh syndrome, and his muscle biopsy demonstrated complex I and III deficiencies.
Two younger brothers presented with a similar phenotype.

Developmental delay
She did not reach the expected developmental milestones at 9 and 12 months. In addition, she did not speak until 5 years old (Fig 1C D). 
A MRI scan at 5 years revealed basal ganglia calcification and changes in the cerebral peduncles . 
CSF and serum lactate were elevated, and muscle biopsy showed complex I on the low end of normal range.
Other: developed precocious puberty.At ag e 17 years, she developed oculogyric episodes over a few months, headache, tremor, bilateral myoclonic jerks and pain in lower limbs, cardiomyopathy.
An EEG revealed excessive polyrhythmic appearances with no evidence of epileptiform activity. NCS showed mild to moderate sensory neuropathy (predominantly in the lower limbs)

At age 27 years she had ophthalmoplegia, a diminished facial expression, bradykinesia and rigidity in both arms.
MRI revealed hyperintensities in the mesencephalon, thalamus, substantia nigra, colliculus inferior and colliculus superior. 
She had elevated CSF lactate .
Four months after the initial presentation, she was admitted to hospital with pneumonia and became respiratory distress necessitating mechanical ventilation. She developed dysphagia, bilateral facial weakness and quadriplegia. 
brain imaging showed a progression of the hyperintense lesions in the mesencephalon and the medulla compared to the previous scans. 
After 3 months her ophthalmoplegia and facial weakness resolved almost completely, her limbs were much stronger, and she was able to walk with some support, but still required ventilatory support. The resolution of MRI abnormalities mirrored the clinical improvement.

Leigh syndrome and parkinsonism

Upon presentation: Slight nuchal rigidity, gait ataxia, hyperreflexia, plantar reflexes in extension, and absence of sensorimotor dysfunction were noticed. Neuroophthalmologic examination showed upward gaze palsy, downward gaze paresis, bilateral miosis not reacting to light and preserved convergence, and ability to abduct the eyes.
Progression: In the 3 weeks after admission, a deterioration of gait and balance was seen, as well as lack of initiative. Slight speech difficulties (hypophonia and tachylalia) were detected, as well as slow movements.
MRI: bMRI showed nonacute lacunar or cystic T2-hyperintense lesions in the basal ganglia (caudate nucleus and putamen) and remarkable acute symmetrical fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR)-/T2-hyperintense lesions in the mesencephalon, suggestive of Leigh disease (figure, A and B). 
Elevated lactate: CSF analysis on the first admission was normal, except for increased lactate concentration (38 mg/dL, normal 10–22), confirmed a few days later (33 mg/dL). Serum lactate, vitamin B12, and thiamin were normal.
At two year follow up: extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS) were seen characterized by marked hypomimia, mild bradykinesia, difficulties with fine motor skills, discrete cogwheel rigidity, diminished arm swing amplitude, disturbed finger movements, slight dysdiadochokinesis, and hypophonia.
Other: Mild left ventricular hypertrophy (interventricular septum and left ventricular posterior wall thickness of 13 mm). The urinary bladder was atonic. 
In cultured skin fibroblasts, a significantly decreased activity of complex IV was found (complex I not assessed) (table)

Leigh syndrome

Failure to thrive, developmental delay, bilateral strabismus, horizontal nystagmus, ptosis, ophthalmoplegia, temporal optic pallor, optic atrophy. Brain MRI at 2 years showed symmetric increased T2 signals in the corticospinal tracts in the brainstem (midbrain and medulla

figure 2A), and the T1-weighted image showed a mildly thick and dysmorphic corpus callosum (figure 2B) while the spine MRI was normal. Serum lactate was markedly elevated. Short stature, progressive spastic paraparesis, spasticity in lower extremeties, brisk deep tendon reflexes, clonus and extensor plantar response, right hydronephrosis, mild-to-moderate ID. Brain MRI at 7 years revealed hyperintense signals in the bilateral periventricular white matter, bilateral putamen, right side of medial thalamus or periaqueductal area of the midbrain (figure 2D–F). Primary ovarian failure at age 11 years. Muscle biopsy at the age of 4 years revealed decreased complex IV activity (cytochrome c oxidase: 750 nmol/min/mg and 770 nmol/min/mg in II-3 and II-4, respectively, normal range: 1413±518 nmol/min/mg), while the other mitochondrial complexes (I, II, III and V) were normal (see online supplementary methods and table S1).
inclusion criteria: developmental delay, neuroimaging findings in bilateral brainstem and midbrain, elevated lactate, decreased complex IV activity in muscle

Leigh syndrome, optic atrophy, and ophthalmoplegia

From age 1, the patient had failure to thrive with swallowing problems and regression of psychomotor development. At 18 months, psychomotor retardation, ataxia, and ptosis were found. An ophthalmological examination showed bilateral paresis of N. abducens. At age 3 years, nystagmus was found, and at age 5 years, severe optic atrophy and decreased vision were found. A cerebral MRI at age 2 years showed bilateral lesions in the thalami, brain stem, and medulla spinalis, and a diagnosis of Leigh syndrome (MIM#256000) was made. At 8 years, her height and weight were normal for her age. She was hypermobile with ataxia and used a wheelchair. She had severely reduced vision with optic atrophy and ophthalmoplegia. Plasma lactate was on one occasion elevated to 2.7 mmol/l. Urine organic acids, urine amino acids, and plasma amino acids were normal. Decreased cytochrome c oxidase (COX, complex IV) activity in fibroblasts (COX/citrate synthase 0.27 versus control mean 0.68, control range 0.4–1.1). The patient died at age 8. No images presented in paper.

inclusion criteria: developmental delay/regression, bilateral lesions of thalami/brainstem on MRI, elevated plasma lactate

optic atrophy and Leigh syndrome–like disease

Developmental delay, including speech
nystagmus by 9 mo. At 2 yr: decreased visual acuity
normal brain MRI and spectroscopy. At 7 yr: drop in vision of left eye
abduction limitation of the right eye
decreased movement of left side of face
lumbering gait with modified Gowers maneuver

bilateral intention tremor and dysmetria. MRI revealed areas of T2 prolongation involving the bilateral periaqueductal gray matter, extending inferiorly along the pontine tegmentum and facial colliculi, terminating in the dorsal aspect of the central medulla (Fig. 1). These areas were not associated with brainstem expansion or volume loss. Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) demonstrated mixed diffusion characteristics with areas of low and high diffusivity. Magnetic resonance spectroscopy showed nonspecific mildly elevated choline–N-acetylaspartate ratio within the region of signal abnormality of the right midbrain. This constellation of findings was suggestive of Leigh syndrome. Repeat brain MRI at 8 years of age showed a decrease in the extent of the T2 prolongation previously identified within the midline medulla, ventral pons, and periaqueductal regions, with extension into the midbrain around the red nucleus. The optic nerve sheaths remained dilated bilaterally (Fig. 2). Plasma and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) lactate levels were normal, as was pyruvic acid in whole blood and plasma creatine kinase levels. Lumbar puncture revealed decreased glucose of 56 mg/dL (normal: 60–80 mg/dL) and low protein of 13.8 mg/dL (normal: 15.0–45.0 mg/dL), with normal CSF amino acids, neurotransmitters, sialic acid, and 5-methyltetrahydrofolate. Activity of complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase) in fibroblasts was reduced to below 40% of normal controls in Case 1 and below 30% in younger sibling. Abnormal mitochondria in fibroblasts on EM. No autopsy. 
Inclusion criteria: fluctuating neuroimaging abnormalities in bilateral midbrain/medulla, developmental delay, reduced OXPHOS activity

Leigh-like syndrome, optic atrophy, and ophthalmoplegia

Pregnancy, delivery, and initial development were normal. Nystagmus was noted at birth. At age 3 years, a divergent squint arose. Ophthalmological examination revealed optic atrophy. MRI of the brain was normal at that time. From the age of 7 years, slow deterioration of vision and motor and cognitive function occurred. He also developed urinary stress incontinence. Neurological examination at the age of 21 years revealed optic atrophy with diminished vision, divergent squint, irregular nystagmus, incomplete external ophthalmoplegia with limited eye movements, and bulbar paresis. Examination of the arms and legs revealed atrophy and weakness of the distal muscles, as well as distal decrease in sensory function. There were no evident signs of a cerebellar ataxia. At the age of 22 years, he developed a respiratory tract infection, respiratory insufficiency, and paralytic ileus. MRI of the brain revealed extensive signal abnormalities in the brain stem, especially the midbrain and medulla, with restricted diffusion and enhancement after contrast. There were also signal abnormalities in the cerebellar white matter and thalamus. Magnetic resonance spectroscopy of the brain stem revealed highly elevated lactate. He died a few months later.
inclusion criteria: signal abnormalities in brainstem, midbrain, thalamus, elevated lactate, developmental delay/regression unclear

Normal early motor milestones, except for tiptoe walking. Developed visual problems at 5 years. Aches in legs and gait problems. Learning difficulties, with poor concentration and memory. Metabolic and CSF studies normal. Brain MRI at age 6y was normal, but detected bilateral, symmetric hyperintensities in the posterior pons and medullary region at 14y and 22y, resembling Leigh’s disease (Fig. 1E). Electrophysiology at age 11y showed widespread lower motor neuron lesion. Muscle biopsy at the age of 10y revealed several cytochrome c oxidase (COX)-deficient fibers following histochemical analysis and biochemical evidence of complex IV deficiency at 40% of control values

the activities of complexes I, II and III were all normal.Clinical examination at age 22 y revealed bilateral optic atrophy but normal eye movements. He had spasticity and bilaterally increased deep tendon reflexes with positive Babinski sign, but absent ankle jerks. He had severely atrophic muscles in the arms and legs with distal predominance and foot drop, and needed bilateral splints. He had no sensory or vegetative symptoms and no ataxia. He had mild intellectual disability.

adult-onset Leigh-like disease

As an infant, reached developmental milestones appropriately. Presented to acute care at 45 years following a fall at home. Neurophysiological evaluation was consistent with an axonal neuropathy. On examination the patient had bilateral ptosis, horizontal gaze paresis and bilateral optic atrophy. He had mild facial muscle weakness, palatal myoclonus, a reduced gag reflex and dysarthria. There was severe distal muscle wasting of upper and lower limbs. Upper and lower limb reflexes were pathologically brisk, with the exception of ankle and plantar reflexes, which were absent. Initial neuropathy screen including serum protein electrophoresis, copper, caeruloplasmin, acylcarnitines, carnitine, phytanic acid, very long chain fatty acids, -fetoprotein and urinary organic acids were normal. Serum lactate was 3.7 mmol/L (normal range <2.0 mmol/l). MRI of the head showed extensive swelling and abnormal high T2-weighted signal changes centrally within the medulla and dorsal pons. Additional small foci of restricted diffusion were seen within the brainstem and the cerebellar hemispheres (Fig. 1). Brain MRS showed a lactate peak, with reduced NAA and raised choline in a voxel centred over the brainstem. Combined these radiological findings were consistent with a diagnosis of Leigh syndrome. Histochemical  analysis  of oxidative enzymes, including succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) and cytochrome c oxidase (COX), in muscle revealed a generalised decrease in COX activity throughout the biopsy sample, confirmed by the sequential COX-SDH  assay (Fig. 2A). Spectrophotometric assays of mitochondrial respiratory chain enzyme activities revealed a severe (approximately 20% of control values) and isolated COX  (CIV) deficiency in the muscle (Fig. 2B).
inclusion criteria: neuroimaging findings in medulla, pons, and brainstem, elevated MRS lactate, reduced OXPHOS activity in muscle but no developmental delay

"Severe cerebral atrophy, age at onset ranged from antenatal to 3 months after birth,  microcephaly at birth, indicative of antenatal disease onset, severe microcephaly at latest examination, tachypnea due to metabolic acidosis in the neonatal period, no eye contact or lost it soon after birth, no motor milestones and no speech, epilepsy.

"A 17-year-old boy presented with complaints of swaying and falls. He had been well until the age of 12 years, when he developed insidious onset and progressive difficulty in walking. He would sway from side to side and had to walk with his feet wide apart. He had difficulty turning corners and frequent falls. He also developed tremulousness of his hands whenever he tried to hold objects. His handwriting gradually deteriorated. Cerebellar signs in the form of gait ataxia, dysmetria, dysdiadokokinesis, and intention tremor were present. Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain showed extensive, inhomogeneous signal abnormalities in the cerebral white matter, mainly located in the periventricular and deep cerebral white
matter, with sparing of the subcortical U-fibers (Figure 1). Within the brain stem, there were signal abnormalities in the pyramidal tracts, medial lemniscus, and superior and inferior cerebellar peduncles. There were also signal abnormalities in the mesencephalic trigeminal tracts and the subcortical cerebellar whitematter (Figure 2). There was no postcontrast enhancement. The diffusion-weighted images showed evidence of inhomogeneous restriction of the diffusion in part of the lesions. The spinal cord images showed signal intensity abnormalities located in the
dorsal columns and the lateral corticospinal tracts over the entire extent of the cord (Figures 3 and 4). The magnetic resonance spectroscopy of the abnormal white matter showed mildly reduced N-acetylaspartate levels. Lactate was not elevated. The imaging findings were characteristic of leukoencephalopathy with brain stem and spinal cord involvement and elevated lactate."
Inclusion criteria: Signal abnormalities in: the cerebral white matter, either in homogeneous and spotty or homogeneous and confluent, relatively sparing the U-fibers
the dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts of the spinal cord

NAXD: This is a German girl with early developmental delay, poor balance, and seizures​. At 1y, she had further regression with fever and ultimately had no spontaneous movements. She also had skin redness, blistering, and peeling, and died at 22m.

This is a boy who was healthy until 3y7m​ when he sustained head injury followed by behavioral changes and unsteady gait. He had subsequent illnesses and further deterioration​. He developed skin lesions (found to be subepidermal skin blistering with extensive epiderma necrosis), intractable seizures, dystonia, chorea, ophthalmoplegia, spastic quadriplegia, profound bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, and pancytopenia​. He died at 4y.

This was a girl who was healthy until 14m​ when she developed progressive dystonia, irritability, diarrhea, and pancytopenia with skin rash (epidermal necrosis with re-epithelialization, vacuolar damage of the basal membrane and dermo-epidermal detachment)​. She improved but remained ataxic, hypotonic, and had ophthalmoplegia​. Over next couple years, she experienced 6-7 episodes of severe dystonia with loss of skills (sometimes with fever)​. She died at 3y10m.

This is an Indian girl healthy until 2.5y ​when she developed  vomiting followed by unsteady gait, intermittent jerky movements, and lethargy​. She was also found to have dilated cardiomyopathy. 
She recovered, worsened, and recovered several times before succumbing several months later​. Of note, her older sister had similar course and was also homozygous for the NAXD variant.

This is a Japanese boy with rapid progression at 7y​. At 1y, he had bronchitis, anemia, and high CK​. By 4y, he had poor communication, clumsiness, and repetitive behaviors​. At 7y, he had gastrointestinal illness with vomiting and lactic acidosis. He died suddenly and autopsy revealed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

This is a Chinese boy with characteristic course​. He was healthy until 2y2m when he had an URI with fever and developed skin lesions (extensive necrosis of the epidermis and detachment from the underlying dermis with a few lymphocytes around the vessels in the upper dermis), involuntary movements, and regression​. He was treated with mitochondrial medicine cocktail (biotin, Vit B1/B2/C/E, CoQ10, idebenone, carnitine) and improved after 2w (resolution of skin lesions, brain atrophy, and basal ganglia lesions)
then at 3y10m had another febrile illness with return of skin lesions, unsteadiness, and regression
and basal ganglia lesions. This then resolved again after several months after being treated with high dose B3. He has had no new episodes at 5y.

This is a girl who was healthy until 1y​ when, following acute gastroenteritis, she was lethargic, decreased appetite, hypotonic, anemic and died shortly after​. Of note, her sibling had mild developmental delay, mild anemia recurrent episodes of fever and died after an episode of vomiting and lethargy at 1y7m.

This is a British boy healthy until 3m ​when he had a viral infection followed by developmental regression and infantile spasms​. He also had extensive progressive skin lesions (acute, full thickness epidermal necrosis best classified as toxic epidermal necrolysis), pancytopenia, neutropenia, bilateral cataracts, and left ventricular hypertrophy. He died shortly after onset of features.

NR < 1.33). Metabolic work-up (plasma and CSF amino acids, organic acids in urine) was unremarkable except for mildly increased excretion of pyruvate in urine. Electroencephalogram showed slower brain activity (4-5 Hz), but without epileptic discharges. 

Brain MRI showed extensive signal abnormalities in the cortex, subcortical cerebral white matter, caudate nucleus, putamen, pons, middle
cerebellar peduncles and medulla oblongata. Moreover, cerebellar edema and cervical myelopathy were seen (Figure 2). Diffusion-weighted images showed diffusion restriction in large areas of the cerebral cortex. 

Due to clinical presentation and symmetrical basal ganglia involvement, treatment with a mitochondrial cocktail was started (L-arginine, coenzyme Q10, vitamins C, B1 and B2).

Muscle biopsy showed myopathic changes, while respiratory chain enzyme and pyruvate dehydrogenase activities in fresh frozen muscle were normal. During the following month she was in coma and had tracheostomy. After one month of mechanical ventilation she was successfully weaned from ventilator. At that point she reacted on painful stimuli, but still had disturbed consciousness with hyperkinesia of face muscles, extremity tremor, nystagmus and rigidity of upper limbs. Control MRI performed in one month intervals showed global brain atrophy with thin cerebral cortex, reduced white matter and signal abnormalities in the caudate nucleus and putamen. The brainstem and cerebellum were relatively preserved (Figure 2 andFigure S1).

 Since thiamine transporter deficiency was suspected, thiamine was increased to 200 mg bid per day (previously 100 mg), biotin was added to the cocktail, and antiepileptic treatment was modified (clonazepam for the treatment of hyperkinesia), thereafter she markedly improved. In the following days hyperkinesia almost disappeared, she started to fixate objects and respond to her parents. During the next weeks her muscle tone was improved and she could sit unsupported. About three months after first crisis, her condition worsened again, she became hypotonic with more pronounced strabismus, tremor and psychomotor restlessness. She had short episodes of fever, and erythematous rash on intertriginous and perigenital areas, hands, and feet. 

MRI performed at that point showed thin cerebral cortex with diffusely abnormal signal, cerebral white matter atrophy, and mild signal
abnormalities in the caudate nucleus and putamen. MRS showed increased choline and mildly increased lactate in deep white matter and normal metabolite peaks in basal ganglia. 

Soon, she again developed respiratory insufficiency and a deep coma from which she never recovered. CT scan revealed hypodensity of mesencephalon, pons, and medulla oblongata with brain edema, herniation of cerebellar tonsils with enlarged IV ventricle and hypertensive hydrocephalus. External ventricular drain was placed. CSF lactate was elevated (5.0 mmol/L

NR < 2.2 mmol/L) while blood lactate was normal or close to normal. In the following days skin changes worsened and she had sharply demarcated edematous and erythematous rash on feet, hands, limbs, and areas around mouth and eyes (Figure 2). Skin biopsy showed focal keratosis and normal dermis without inflammation. Clinically, she had signs of systemic inflammatory reaction. Immunological and microbiological work-up was
unremarkable. She received intravenous immunoglobulins (2g/kg) and pulse doses of steroids but there was no obvious benefit. She lost all her hair. After the skin peeled off the skin changes resolved. Further clinical course was complicated with C. difficile colitis and sepsis. 

Control brain MRI three weeks after deterioration showed diffuse brain edema, especially in cerebellum and brain stem as well as cervical myelopathy (Figure 2). After 6 weeks ventricular drainage was taken out and there was no need to place ventriculoperitoneal shunt. Further course was complicated by episodes of hemodynamic instability probably of central origin and sepsis. Last brain MRI was performed two months after deterioration and showed diffuse atrophy, reduced diffusion due to cytotoxic edema of brain stem and cerebellar white matter, and extensive cystic alterations of pons, cerebellar peduncles, pontomesencephalic and pontomedullary junctions. 

The girl died at the age of 24 months due to sepsis which led to irreversible circulatory failure. Autopsy showed periventricular and corticosubcortical encephalomalacia with massive brain edema.

Leigh syndrome: Confirmed Neuropathological diagnosis of Leigh syndrome. Normal neonatal period, early hypotonia, At 10 months of age, he was referred for evaluation of the cause of his retarded development and hypotonia. His blood and cerebrospinal fluid lactate were elevated: 8.6 and 4.9 mmol/l (ref 0.5–2.2mmol/l). Cerebral MRI showed symmetrical lesions in especially the basal ganglia and substantia nigra. Ultrasound suggested some hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. During one such episode of convulsions, he experienced a severe cardiac respiratory arrest, X-ray of the thorax showed massive pleural fluid, most probably as a result of cardiac decompensation by hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.  Patient died at 23 months of age.  At autopsy, besides the already known cardiomyopathy, degenerative abnormalities were reported in the liver, spleen, kidneys, and pancreas. Also saw abnormalities in the brain: in the thalamus, the pons, and also in the hippocampus degradation of myeline, decreased myelinization, proliferation of vessels, some gliosis, and some foam cell response were also reported.
Cultured fibroblasts and fresh muscle tissue of the index patient revealed a severely reduced complex I activity (Table 1).

Complex I deficiency:  Meets GCEP diagnostic criteria for  Leigh syndrome spectrum.
Summary in Table 2. Raised blood lactate, raised CSF lactate, Complex 1 activity <25%, lactate raised in the brain (MRS), loss of abilities, muscular hypotonia, symmetrical basal ganglia lesions, symmetrical brainstem lesions, progressive course.

Leigh syndrome spectrum: Confirmed Neuropathological diagnosis of Leigh syndrome.
At the age of 2 years and 8 months, after a febrile illness, he presented acute-onset ataxia with severe hyposthenia and inability to stand and walk. First brain MRI was performed at that age and showed rather symmetrical lesions of the putamina and globi pallidi, characterized by swelling, necrotic changes, and mixed cytotoxic and vasogenic edema. Asymmetric focal lesions of the caudate nuclei were also noted, as well as focal involvement of the left cerebral peduncle (Fig. 1). MR spectroscopy detected a lactate doublet peak. On further clinical examination noted generalized hypotonia with muscular hypotrophy especially in the lower limbs

tendon reflexes were slightly increased, and oscillatory nystagmus was present. Gait ataxia and clumsiness were also observed. The psychomotor evaluation showed a moderate delay in his development. Plasma lactate was slightly above normal range (39.2 mg/dl, normal range 8–22 mg/dl). Strongly suggestive of LS. 
A second MRI performed at 3 years and 6 months, showed the chronic evolution of the putaminal lesions and the presence of new symmetric lesions of the caudate heads and Meynert basal nuclei and asymmetric lesions at the level of the right globus pallidus and cerebral peduncle. MR spectroscopy, performed on the right caudate nucleus head, revealed a lactate doublet peak (Figure 2)..
Muscle biopsy biochemical anlysis showed reduction of complexes I (1.12 mmol/min/g tissue

normal range 1.56–2.60), I + II (0.08 mmol/min/g tissue,normal range 0.11–0.25), and II + III (0.02 mmol/min/gtissue, normal range 0.05–0.08). 
Family history of disease : paternal cousin who died at the age of 4 months for an unspecified cardiomyopathy, two maternal cousins manifesting an unspecified myopathy, and another cousin affected by an early-onset epileptic encephalopathy.

Motor developmental delay, neurodevelopmental delay, seizures, feeding difficulties, elevated lactate, complex I defect.

NDUFA11:
patient noted to have an abnormal MRI and seizures (no EEG data provided)
Part of large cohort of Chinese patients with neonatal encephalopathy. The inclusion criterion was a diagnosis of NE.6 The diagnosis of NE was based on the following criteria: (a) the gestational age is greater than 35 weeks
(b) a subnormal level of consciousness or seizures or difficulty with initiating and maintaining respiration or depression of muscle tone and reflexes

(c) abnormal brain MRI features or abnormal EEG features. Newborns included in this study need to meet the first criteria, and at least one of the other two criteria. Abnormal brain MRI features included signal abnormalities in white matter, gray matter, or both, hypogenesis of the brain, focal encephalomalacia, edema, delayed myelination or hemorrhage

Patients Patient A-II-1, B-II-2, and B-II-4: at 10 to 24 hours of age the patients became apneic and
hypotensive with severe metabolic acidosis and hyperlactatemia (pH 6.80–6.90

N 2.1mM). Echocardiogram, normal at birth, disclosed biventricular hyperplasia on the second week of life and the patients died at 6 to 40 days of age because of intractable acidosis.
Enzymatic activities of the five respiratory chain complexes were determined in isolated muscle mitochondria from Patients A-II-1, B-II-2, and C-IV-1, and in isolated fibroblast mitochondria from Patient B-II-2 by standard spectrophotometric methods and normalized to citrate synthase.10 The activity of complex I in muscle mitochondria, determined as rotenone-sensitive NADH: cytochrome c oxidoreductase, NADH:ubiquinone reductase, and NADH:ferricyanide reductase, was reduced to 4 to 10%, 13 to 27%, and 19 to 39% of the control mean, respectively. In mitochondria isolated from fibroblasts, NADH:ubiquinone reductase activity was reduced to 45% of the control mean. The activities of the other complexes were within the reference range.

Patients C-IV-1, C-IV-2, and C-IV-7: early psychomotor development was slow. Repeated examinations demonstrated acquired microcephaly, marked generalized hypotonia, muscle weakness, and paucity of voluntary movements. Hearing was normal, but visual fixation was impaired with nystagmus in one patient and bilateral optic atrophy in another. Convulsive disorder appeared at 4 months in one patient. Echocardiogram at birth was normal, but hypertrophy of the myocardial walls and septum (wall thickness, 6mm

normal for age, 2.5–3mm) with mild dilatation of the left ventricle was present at 3 to 4 months of age. Plasma lactate level was increased (3.2–10mM), and the patients suffered from exacerbation of the acidosis during intercurrent
infections. The electroencephalogram was normal, and brain computed tomography scan performed in two patients demonstrated generalized brain atrophy. Two patients died at
18 months and 4 years of age during an intercurrent infection. One patient was alive at 6 months at the time of writing this report. 
Enzymatic activities of the five respiratory chain complexes were determined in isolated muscle mitochondria from Patient C-IV-1. The activity of complex I in muscle mitochondria, determined as rotenone-sensitive NADH: cytochrome c oxidoreductase, NADH:ubiquinone reductase, and NADH:ferricyanide reductase, was reduced to 19 to 39% of the control
mean. The activities of the other complexes were within the reference range.
